word_list.pronunciation: hˈoʊld bˈɛɹ hæv ɐ ɡɹˈʌdʒ ɐɡˈɛnst ˌɛsbˈiː slˈæʃ ˌɛstˌiːˈeɪtʃ

senses.definition: to hold negative feelings toward a person who has done one wrong in the past

examples.x: She still holds a grudge against her sister for borrowing her favorite dress without asking.
Despite their efforts to resolve the issue, he continues to bear a grudge against his coworker for taking credit for his idea.
Having a grudge against her former best friend, she found it hard to trust new people in her life.
It's time to forgive and forget; otherwise, you'll continue to hold a grudge, and it will only harm your own peace of mind.

senses.definition: to deliberately keep causing problems for someone or criticizing them

examples.x: Ever since that argument at the office last week, it seems like the boss has it in for Sarah.
I don't know why she has it in for me, but she's been giving me the cold shoulder all day.
It's clear that the coach has it in for Tom because he keeps benching him for no apparent reason.
Don't cross paths with Alex; he has it in for anyone who questions his authority.
The teacher always seems to have it in for Mark, giving him extra homework assignments for no good reason.

senses.definition: an attitude that shows one is angry or resentful because of an unfair behavior that they have experienced

examples.x: John always walked around with a chip on his shoulder, ready to argue with anyone who disagreed with him.
Sarah's constant need to prove herself came from the chip on her shoulder after being underestimated by her colleagues.
The new employee joined the team without a chip on their shoulder, making it easier to adapt to the workplace culture.
Instead of carrying a chip on his shoulder, Mike decided to focus on self-improvement and personal growth.
Despite his achievements, Tom couldn't let go of the chip on his shoulder from his challenging childhood.

senses.definition: a strong feeling of bitterness or hatred toward someone because of a past dispute or disagreement

examples.x: Despite their efforts to reconcile, there was still bad blood between the two neighbors after the property dispute.
The political debate was tense, with the candidates displaying bad blood stemming from previous clashes.
The family reunion was awkward due to the bad blood between two estranged siblings.
In the competitive business world, there's often bad blood between rival companies vying for the same market share.
The history of bad blood between the two nations made diplomatic negotiations challenging.

word_list.pronunciation: ɡɛt ɔːɹ stˈɑːɹt ˈɔf ɑːnðə ɹˈɔŋ fˈʊt

senses.definition: to have an unsuccessful or unpleasant beginning in a relationship or activity

examples.x: They got off on the wrong foot after a misunderstanding.
His rude comment made them get off on the wrong foot.
They started off on the wrong foot but later became friends.
The project started off on the wrong foot due to poor planning.
Their business deal got off on the wrong foot due to a communication error.

senses.definition: to intentionally and rudely display an offensive hand gesture, involving raising the middle finger while extending the others downward, as a sign of strong disapproval, annoyance, or disrespect toward someone

examples.x: When the driver cut him off in traffic, he couldn't resist giving them the bird.
After hearing his rude comments, she was tempted to give him the bird, but she decided to stay composed.
Instead of arguing with his neighbor, he simply gave him the bird and walked away to avoid further confrontation.

senses.definition: to hate someone to an extreme degree

examples.x: After the betrayal, she couldn't hide the fact that she hated his guts.
I can't stand the guy; I hate his guts for what he did to me.

word_list.pronunciation: hæv ɐ bˈoʊn tə pˈɪk wɪð ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to have a problem with someone or be annoyed with them and want to tell them all about it

examples.x: After the meeting, Jane told her colleague, "I have a bone to pick with you about the way you presented our project.

word_list.pronunciation: pˌʊt stˈɪk ðə nˈaɪf ɪn ˌɪntʊ ˌɛsbˈiː

senses.definition: to emotionally harm someone through a significant act of betrayal or hurtful actions

examples.x: The tabloids are known for putting the knife into celebrities, often reporting scandalous stories to damage their public image.

senses.definition: to have an open conversation with someone to resolve the negative feelings with them that are caused by disagreements or misunderstandings

examples.x: After their argument, they decided to clear the air by sitting down and discussing their differences.
We cleared the air after our disagreement yesterday.
In the office, the manager called a staff meeting to clear the air and address any concerns or grievances.
Before the presentation, he will clear the air about the project's progress.
The team held a meeting to clear the air about the recent project delays and improve collaboration.

senses.definition: to build a good relationship with each other again after having disagreements about something

examples.x: After years of arguing, the two neighbors decided to bury the hatchet and become friends again.
The business partners realized that harboring old grudges wouldn't help their company, so they agreed to bury the hatchet and move forward.
The celebrity couple announced that they had buried the hatchet and were committed to making their marriage work after a publicized separation.
The long-standing rivalry between the two schools came to an end when they decided to bury the hatchet and focus on academic excellence.
Even though they had been in a bitter dispute over property boundaries, the neighbors decided to bury the hatchet and find an amicable solution.

senses.definition: to argue or fight until a disagreement is resolved

examples.x: After hours of heated negotiations, the two companies finally decided to duke it out and reach a compromise on the merger deal.
The siblings had been arguing about the family inheritance for weeks until they decided to duke it out during a family meeting.
Instead of letting their differences fester, the coworkers decided to duke it out in a constructive discussion to find common ground.
